content.text: What a Claude CoP Is — and Is Not

A Community of Practice (CoP) for Claude practitioners is a voluntary, cross-functional group of employees who share a domain of interest (using Claude effectively and responsibly) and a commitment to deepening their collective knowledge. It is not a governance body — it does not set policy. It is not a training department — it does not own the curriculum. It is not a steering committee — it does not approve investment decisions. The CoP sits alongside these structures and accelerates the informal knowledge-sharing, peer learning, and use-case discovery that formal channels cannot reach.

The value proposition of a Claude CoP is speed and credibility. Employees learn more from a peer showing a clever prompt pattern in a Slack message than from a policy document. A CoP creates the organisational scaffolding for that peer learning to happen at scale — deliberately, sustainably, and with governance guardrails.

A charter is the foundational document that establishes the CoP's purpose, scope, membership, governance, and operating cadence. It prevents the CoP from drifting, from being co-opted for purposes it was not designed to serve, and from collapsing when founding members leave. The charter is a living document — reviewed quarterly, not set in stone.

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_cop_charter(
    organisation_name: str,
    cop_scope: str,
    founding_members: list[str],
    executive_sponsor: str,
    primary_goals: list[str],
    out_of_scope: list[str],
    membership_criteria: str,
) -> str:
    """Draft a CoP charter for a Claude practitioners community."""
    prompt = f"""Draft a Community of Practice charter for a Claude practitioners CoP.

Organisation: {organisation_name}
CoP scope: {cop_scope}
Founding members: {", ".join(founding_members)}
Executive sponsor: {executive_sponsor}
Primary goals (3-5):
{chr(10).join(f"- {g}" for g in primary_goals)}
Explicitly out of scope:
{chr(10).join(f"- {o}" for o in out_of_scope)}
Membership: {membership_criteria}

Draft a charter with these sections:
1. Purpose and value proposition (2 sentences)
2. Scope (what the CoP does and explicitly does not do)
3. Membership (who can join, roles, responsibilities)
4. Governance (lead, core council, decision-making)
5. Operating cadence (meeting types, channels, knowledge management)
6. Success metrics (3-4 measurable outcomes, reviewed quarterly)
7. Charter review cadence
8. Relationship to governance bodies (IT, Legal, HR, L&D)

Tone: clear, professional, not legalistic. Under 600 words."""

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=900,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


charter = generate_cop_charter(
    organisation_name="Meridian Financial Services",
    cop_scope="Use of Claude across all business units — prompting excellence, safe use patterns, use-case discovery, peer learning",
    founding_members=["Lead Data Scientist", "Support Operations Manager", "Senior Software Engineer", "HR Business Partner", "Change Lead"],
    executive_sponsor="Chief Operating Officer",
    primary_goals=[
        "Curate and share effective prompt patterns across business units",
        "Surface emerging use cases for governance review",
        "Provide peer support for new Claude users",
        "Feed learning into the training curriculum improvement cycle",
        "Build a network of advocates who accelerate adoption wave by wave",
    ],
    out_of_scope=[
        "Setting acceptable use policy (owned by Governance committee)",
        "Approving new Claude deployments (owned by IT Architecture)",
        "Managing training curriculum (owned by L&D)",
    ],
    membership_criteria="Open to any certified Claude user who commits to at least one contribution per quarter",
)
print(charter[:800])

The core council is the small group (five to nine members) that runs the CoP's operations. Unlike the broader membership, council members have defined responsibilities and time commitments. Rotate council seats annually to prevent the CoP from becoming the domain of a fixed in-group.
Sets agenda, chairs monthly showcase, represents CoP to steering committee, manages sponsor relationship
Maintains prompt library, wiki, and resource repository; tags and categorises member contributions
Manages change champion roster, assigns champions to new waves, collects field intelligence
Attends governance committee meetings, flags policy questions from CoP to committee, feeds policy updates back to CoP
Feeds CoP insights into curriculum, coordinates with training team, reviews curriculum updates with CoP members

The CoP Lead role carries the most risk of burnout. Ensure the organisation formally recognises the time commitment — a 2-3 hour per week role should be visible in the individual's objectives, not treated as an informal extra.

The monthly showcase is the CoP's primary event — a sixty-minute session where members share real use cases, prompt patterns, and lessons learned. The quality of the showcase determines whether members keep showing up. Claude can help generate themed agendas that balance skill-building, use-case sharing, and open discussion.

def generate_monthly_showcase_agenda(
    month: str,
    theme: str,
    featured_use_cases: list[dict],
    governance_update: str | None,
    new_members_count: int,
) -> str:
    """Generate a structured monthly CoP showcase agenda."""
    use_cases_text = "\n".join(
        f"- {uc['presenter']} ({uc['role']}): {uc['topic']} — {uc['estimated_minutes']} min"
        for uc in featured_use_cases
    )
    prompt = f"""Create a 60-minute Community of Practice showcase agenda.

Month: {month}
Theme: {theme}
New members joining: {new_members_count}
Governance update to share: {governance_update or "None this month"}

Featured use cases to include:
{use_cases_text}

Format as a timed agenda with:
- Welcome + theme intro (first 5 min)
- New member welcomes if applicable
- Featured use-case presentations
- Open discussion / Q&A
- Governance / policy update (if any)
- Knowledge base highlights (new resources added)
- Close + next month preview

Include facilitator notes for each segment (in brackets after the timing)."""

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=700,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


agenda = generate_monthly_showcase_agenda(
    month="March 2025",
    theme="Making Claude Output Review Faster Without Cutting Corners",
    featured_use_cases=[
        {"presenter": "James Park", "role": "Support Agent", "topic": "My three-step review checklist for ticket drafts", "estimated_minutes": 10},
        {"presenter": "Priya Sharma", "role": "Senior Analyst", "topic": "Using Claude to check Claude — building a second-pass prompt", "estimated_minutes": 12},
    ],
    governance_update="Data classification update: structured financial data now permitted in prompts with explicit anonymisation step",
    new_members_count=7,
)
print(agenda)

The most tangible artefact a Claude CoP produces is a curated prompt library — a repository of reusable, tested, role-annotated prompts that members can adapt for their own tasks. Without a library, every employee rediscovers the same prompt patterns independently; with one, a new starter can be productive in their first week.

def add_prompt_to_library(
    title: str,
    author: str,
    author_role: str,
    use_case_category: str,
    roles_applicable: list[str],
    prompt_template: str,
    example_output: str,
    caveats: list[str],
    date_added: str,
) -> dict:
    """Structure a prompt for inclusion in the CoP prompt library."""
    # Generate a short description using Claude
    description_prompt = f"""Write a 2-sentence description for a prompt library entry.

Title: {title}
Use case: {use_case_category}
Template: {prompt_template[:300]}

Description should explain: what the prompt does, and when to use it.
Return plain text only."""

    from anthropic import Anthropic
    client = Anthropic()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=100,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": description_prompt}],
    )
    
    return {
        "id": f"{use_case_category.lower().replace(' ', '-')}--{title.lower().replace(' ', '-')[:30]}",
        "title": title,
        "description": resp.content[0].text.strip(),
        "author": author,
        "author_role": author_role,
        "category": use_case_category,
        "roles_applicable": roles_applicable,
        "prompt_template": prompt_template,
        "example_output": example_output,
        "caveats": caveats,
        "date_added": date_added,
        "upvotes": 0,
        "usage_count": 0,
        "review_status": "pending_curator_review",
    }


entry = add_prompt_to_library(
    title="Ticket Summary for Handover",
    author="James Park",
    author_role="Support Agent",
    use_case_category="Customer Support",
    roles_applicable=["Support Agent", "Support Team Lead"],
    prompt_template="""Summarise this support ticket for handover to a different agent.
Ticket: {{TICKET_CONTENT}}
Include: (1) issue in one sentence, (2) steps already taken, (3) customer sentiment, (4) recommended next action.
Keep to under 100 words. Do not include any customer PII beyond first name.""",
    example_output="Customer Jane reports her direct debit was charged twice on 2 March. Agent confirmed the duplicate on the payment system. Customer is frustrated but cooperative. Recommended next action: initiate refund via Finance portal and follow up within 24 hours.",
    caveats=["Replace {{TICKET_CONTENT}} with the full ticket text", "Review output before sending to receiving agent — verify facts"],
    date_added="2025-03-01",
)
print(f"Library entry: {entry['id']}")
print(f"Description: {entry['description']}")

A CoP that is not measured drifts toward becoming an exclusive club for enthusiasts. Track four health indicators quarterly: participation breadth (how many unique members contribute?), knowledge utilisation (how often is the prompt library accessed?), pipeline contribution (how many use cases did the CoP surface for governance review?), and member satisfaction (do members feel the CoP is worth their time?).
% of members who contributed at least once in the quarter
% of members attending at least 2 of 3 monthly showcases
Flat or declining for 2+ consecutive months
Emerging use cases surfaced to governance per quarter
0 (CoP is not in contact with real work)
Launching the CoP: The First 90 Days

The first 90 days of a CoP determine whether it becomes self-sustaining or fades into inactivity. The critical success factor is visible early value: members must experience something useful in their first two sessions, or they will not return for a third. Plan the first three monthly showcases in advance, with high-quality presenters and relevant topics confirmed before any invitations go out.
Establish identity and gather founding members
Charter signed, Slack channel live, prompt library stub published
Overcommitting scope in excitement; keep charter narrow
First public showcase with 3 featured use cases
Showcase recording available on intranet within 48 hours
Low attendance if showcase competes with sprint/quarter-end
First prompt library contributions from members; governance liaison relationship established
5+ prompt library entries, first use case surfaced to governance
Core council burnout if non-council members not contributing

def generate_launch_plan(
    organisation_name: str,
    cop_lead: str,
    executive_sponsor: str,
    target_founding_members: int,
    launch_date: str,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Generate a 90-day CoP launch plan."""
    from anthropic import Anthropic
    import json
    client = Anthropic()
    prompt = f"""Create a 90-day launch plan for a Claude Community of Practice.

Organisation: {organisation_name}
CoP Lead: {cop_lead}
Executive Sponsor: {executive_sponsor}
Target founding members: {target_founding_members}
Launch date: {launch_date}

Generate week-by-week milestones for the first 12 weeks. For each week:
- week: integer (1–12)
- milestone: brief description
- owner: role (CoP Lead, Core Council, L&D Partner, Sponsor, etc.)
- success_indicator: how you know this week's milestone is done
- dependency: what must be true before this milestone starts

Return JSON array of milestone objects."""

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1500,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return json.loads(resp.content[0].text)


plan = generate_launch_plan(
    organisation_name="Meridian Financial Services",
    cop_lead="Dr. Aisha Okonkwo",
    executive_sponsor="Chief Operating Officer",
    target_founding_members=25,
    launch_date="2025-03-03",
)
for milestone in plan[:4]:
    print(f"Week {milestone['week']}: {milestone['milestone']} (Owner: {milestone['owner']})")

Weeks 1–4 should be entirely preparatory. The CoP must not hold its first public event until the charter is signed, the Slack channel is live, and at least three high-quality showcase presenters are confirmed. A poorly-run first showcase is very hard to recover from.

The most common CoP failure mode is not collapse in the first month — it is slow fade in months 10–14. The founding members are busy; the novelty has worn off; new Claude model updates have reduced the urgency of the original knowledge gaps. Build year-two momentum into the operating model from the start.
— revisit purpose, scope, and success metrics every twelve months. A CoP that was founded to support Wave 1 adoption should evolve its purpose as the organisation moves to advanced use cases.
— annual rotation of core council seats brings fresh energy and prevents the CoP from becoming an in-group. Departing council members become senior contributors who mentor new council members.
— give each quarter a focus (e.g. 'Q1: Prompt Safety'; 'Q2: Developer Use Cases'; 'Q3: Advanced Workflows') to maintain programme coherence and give members a reason to contribute.

— invite external speakers (Anthropic enterprise team, academic researchers, practitioners from non-competing organisations) twice a year. External perspective regenerates enthusiasm and surfaces ideas the internal community would not generate alone.

— acknowledge top contributors at the annual programme review, in internal comms, and through visible markers (e.g. an 'Advanced Practitioner' badge linked to CoP contribution count). Recognition is the most cost-effective retention mechanism for voluntary communities.
— a CoP must be voluntary. Mandatory membership destroys the intrinsic motivation that drives genuine contribution and produces a community of passive attendees.

— a CoP without visible executive support is perceived as a grassroots hobby group, not an organisational priority. The sponsor should attend at least two showcases per year and reference the CoP in all-hands communications.
Letting the prompt library go stale

— a library with 40 entries, the newest six months old, signals organisational disengagement. Curate aggressively: archive outdated prompts, update ones that break with model changes, highlight new additions monthly.

— a CoP that operates in isolation from the governance committee becomes a source of shadow practice — employees sharing prompt patterns that governance has not reviewed. The Governance Liaison role exists precisely to prevent this.
Single point of failure in the lead

— a CoP where everything depends on one person's energy and knowledge is not a community, it is a fan club. Distribute responsibility through the council structure and ensure the lead's institutional knowledge is documented in the charter and wiki.

A Claude CoP charter and operating model gives voluntary practitioner communities the structure they need to sustain themselves and create genuine value. The charter defines purpose, scope, and governance; the operating model defines who does what and when; the prompt library and monthly showcase make the value tangible. Claude can assist at every stage — drafting the charter, generating showcase agendas, structuring prompt library entries, and producing health reports — while the community itself remains human-led, with membership driven by genuine interest rather than mandate.
